title: ArrayList: How Arrays Grow
subtitle: Discover what happens in memory when a Python list runs out of space and needs to grow.
encouragement: Every Python list you've ever used hides this trick. Python doesn't simply expand the existing memory; instead, it creates a completely new, larger array, copies the existing elements into it, and then discards the old one. Once you see it with your hands, you'll understand why appending is almost always fast but sometimes takes a beat longer.
realWorldExample: Think of a notebook with numbered lines. You write one item per line. When you fill the last line, you can't just glue extra pages on. You grab a bigger notebook, copy everything over, and toss the old one in the recycling bin. Python lists do exactly this behind the scenes.

materials.detail: One short strip (~6 inches) and one longer strip (~12 inches). The short one is your starting array; the long one is the resized array.
To draw index boxes and write values.
About 1 inch each. Write these letters on them: A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H
To symbolically attach the new larger strip when you resize.
This marks the next available slot where a new item would go.

setup: Take your SHORT strip and draw 4 equally-spaced boxes on it. Number them 0 through 3 underneath.
This strip represents an array of capacity 4. It can hold exactly 4 items, no more.
Place your paperclip at index 0. This is where the next item will go. The array is empty; size is 0.
Set your LONG strip and tape aside for now. You'll need them later.
Lay out your letter cards (A through H) nearby.

steps.instruction: Append A to your ArrayList. Place the A card into the next available slot.
steps.action: Place "A" into box 0. Move your paperclip to index 1.
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [A][ ][ ][ ], paperclip at index 1. Size: 1, Capacity: 4.
steps.conceptNote: Appending when there's room is simple: drop the item in, move the pointer. This costs O(1), constant time.

steps.instruction: Append B and C, one at a time.
steps.action: Place "B" in box 1 (move paperclip to 2). Then place "C" in box 2 (move paperclip to 3).
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [A][B][C][ ], paperclip at index 3. Size: 3, Capacity: 4.
steps.conceptNote: Still room left. Each append is O(1). Fast, no drama.

steps.instruction: Append D. Place it in the last empty box.
steps.action: Place "D" in box 3. Move your paperclip past the end of the strip, there's nowhere for it to point.
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [A][B][C][D], paperclip is off the edge. Size: 4, Capacity: 4. The array is FULL.
steps.conceptNote: The array is now at capacity. Size equals capacity. The next append will be different.
steps.hint: Notice: the strip has no more room. What happens when you need to add another item?

steps.instruction: You need to append E, but there's no room. Here's the key: Python does NOT expand the old strip. It creates a brand new, larger array in a completely different place in memory. Grab your LONG strip and draw 8 boxes on it, numbered 0 through 7.
steps.action: Draw 8 boxes on the long strip. Place it NEXT TO (not on top of) the old strip. You now have two arrays: the old full one and the new empty one. They exist in different memory locations.
steps.checkpoint: Two strips side by side: the old strip [A][B][C][D] and a new empty strip [ ][ ][ ][ ][ ][ ][ ][ ].
steps.conceptNote: Python allocates a new array with double the capacity in a different region of memory. This prevents modifying the original memory block and avoids fragmentation. The old array still exists right now; we haven't discarded it yet.

steps.instruction: Now copy every element from the old strip to the new strip, one by one. But here's a critical detail: this is a shallow copy, not a deep copy.
steps.action: Pick up each card from the old strip and place it in the same position on the new strip. A to box 0, B to box 1, C to box 2, D to box 3. Notice: you moved the SAME cards. You didn't rewrite new cards with the same letters.
steps.checkpoint: New strip: [A][B][C][D][ ][ ][ ][ ]. Old strip is now empty.
steps.conceptNote: This copy costs O(n); you had to touch every single element. But understand what "copy" means here: Python copies the references (pointers to objects), not the objects themselves. Your cards represent references. A shallow copy means the new container has its own slots, but the slots point to the exact same objects as the old container. A deep copy would create brand-new copies of every object too, like rewriting each card from scratch.
steps.hint: Use tape to symbolically set the old strip aside. It's been replaced. The new strip IS your array now.

steps.instruction: Pause and think about this: why does shallow vs deep copy matter? Imagine your card A wasn't just the letter "A" but a small envelope with a list inside it, like [1, 2, 3].
steps.action: Imagine you open card A's envelope on the NEW strip and change the list to [1, 2, 99]. Because it was a shallow copy, the original object was shared. If the old strip still existed, opening card A there would ALSO show [1, 2, 99]. Both strips held the same envelope, not copies of it.
steps.conceptNote: A shallow copy creates a new container (new list object, new strip) but the contents are shared with the original. A deep copy creates a new container AND new copies of every nested object inside it. During an ArrayList resize, Python does a shallow copy: new array, same object references. If you need independence between the original and copy, you'd use import copy; copy.deepcopy(lst).
steps.hint: This is one of the trickiest concepts in CS 1134. If you modify a nested list inside a shallow copy, the change appears in the original too, because both point to the same object in memory.

steps.instruction: Now throw away the old strip. Crumple it up, toss it aside, or flip it over. It no longer exists in memory. Then place E into the new strip.
steps.action: Physically discard the old strip (crumple it, push it away, whatever feels satisfying). Then place "E" in box 4 on the new strip. Move your paperclip to index 5.
steps.checkpoint: Only ONE strip remains: [A][B][C][D][E][ ][ ][ ], paperclip at index 5. Size: 5, Capacity: 8. The old strip is gone.
steps.conceptNote: This is garbage collection in action. Once all elements are copied to the new array, nothing references the old array anymore. Python's garbage collector detects this and frees that memory. The old array is gone. That single append of E required: allocate new array (8 slots) + copy 4 items + discard old array + place E. Expensive!

steps.action: Place "F" in box 5 (move paperclip to 6). Place "G" in box 6 (move paperclip to 7).
steps.conceptNote: Back to O(1) appends again. No resize needed. These are cheap.

steps.instruction: Let's count the total work. You appended 7 items (A through G). How many were cheap O(1) appends, and how many triggered a resize?
steps.action: Count: A, B, C, D were O(1). Then E triggered a resize costing 5 operations. Then F, G were O(1). That's 6 cheap appends and 1 expensive one.
steps.conceptNote: Total operations: 6 cheap (1 each) + 1 expensive (5 total) = 11 operations for 7 appends. Average per append: about 1.6 operations. Not bad!
steps.hint: As the array gets bigger, resizes happen less and less often. The cheap appends outnumber the expensive ones by a growing margin.

steps.instruction: This averaging effect has a name: amortized O(1). Imagine spreading the cost of that one expensive resize across all the cheap appends that came before and after it.
steps.conceptNote: "Amortized" means "averaged over time." Each individual append might cost O(1) or O(n), but averaged over many appends, each one costs O(1). The doubling strategy is what makes this work.
steps.hint: Think of it like paying rent. One big payment once a month, spread across 30 days of living. Each day's cost is small.

steps.instruction: Final question: what would happen if instead of doubling, you only added 1 extra box each time you resized?
steps.action: Imagine: capacity 4 fills up, you make a new strip of 5, copy 4 items. Then it fills, you make 6, copy 5. Then 7, copy 6. Every single append triggers a copy!
steps.conceptNote: Adding only 1 slot means you resize on EVERY append after the initial fill. That's O(n) every time. Doubling avoids this because each resize buys you enough room for many future appends.
steps.hint: This is why doubling matters. It's not arbitrary. It's the mathematical trick that makes amortized O(1) possible.

reflection.question: What happens when you append to an ArrayList that's at capacity?
reflection.answer: Python creates a completely new, larger array in a different place in memory (usually double the size), copies all existing elements into it, discards the old array via garbage collection, then places the new item. It does NOT expand the existing array.

reflection.question: Why does the ArrayList double its capacity instead of just adding one more slot?
reflection.answer: Doubling ensures that resizes happen less and less frequently as the array grows. This gives amortized O(1) append performance. Adding just one slot would mean resizing on every single append.

reflection.question: What does 'amortized O(1)' mean in plain language?
reflection.answer: Most appends are instant (O(1)), and the rare expensive resize gets spread out over all the cheap ones. On average, each append costs constant time.

reflection.question: If an ArrayList has size 100 and capacity 128, how many more appends can you do before a resize?
reflection.answer: 28 more appends. The resize happens when size reaches capacity (128). Then it would double to 256.

reflection.question: During a resize, is the copy of elements a shallow copy or a deep copy? Why does this matter?
reflection.answer: It's a shallow copy. Remember: a shallow copy creates a new container (a new list object with its own slots) but the contents are shared with the original. Python copies the references (pointers) to the objects, not the objects themselves. So if the list contains mutable objects like inner lists, the new array and old array point to the exact same objects in memory. Modifying a nested list inside a shallow copy also changes the original, because both point to the same object. A deep copy, by contrast, would create new copies of every nested object too, giving you full independence between the original and the copy.
